title: Decoding Higher Education
text:
While university placement data is public, there is a lack of analytical perspective on how factors like faculty size and location impact a department’s popularity. This project identifies the underlying drivers of success for Industrial Engineering departments in Turkey.
Team Name: [Let It Happen]
Team Members: * Yusuf Bora Çavdar
* Onur Furkan Gök

title: Decoding Higher Education
text:
While university placement data is public, there is a lack of analytical perspective on how factors like faculty size and location impact a department’s popularity. This project identifies the underlying drivers of success for Industrial Engineering departments in Turkey.
Team Name: [Let It Happen]
Team Members: * Yusuf Bora Çavdar
* Onur Furkan Gök

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
Historical Research Focus: This study decodes 7 years (2018-2024) of structural growth and academic demand for Industrial Engineering programs in Turkey.
Hybrid Data Sourcing & Imputation: We successfully integrated live API extraction with static datasets. We engineered a normalization algorithm to eliminate string-mismatches and fully impute missing historical data.
Faculty & Global Reach: Advanced modeling indicates a strong positive correlation between the number of academic staff, international mobility (Erasmus), and the program’s national success rank.
Comprehensive Dynamics: By converting categorical variables into numeric features, our exhaustive correlation matrix conclusively proves that metropolitan location, absolute demand, top-choice placements, and global opportunities all act synergistically to drive academic selectivity.
Code
p1 <- ie_combined %>%
  count(Year, University_Type) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(Year), y = n, fill = University_Type, 
             text = paste("Year:", Year, "<br>Sector:", University_Type, "<br>Programs:", n))) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("State" = "#38bdf8", "Foundation" = "#94a3b8")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Growth of IE Programs (2018-2024)", x = "Academic Year", y = "Number of Active Programs") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p1, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"),
         legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.2))
Code
latest_year <- max(ie_combined$Year, na.rm = TRUE)

p_violin <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year, !is.na(Rank)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = University_Type, y = Rank, fill = University_Type, text = paste("Sector:", University_Type))) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6, color = "transparent") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, fill = "#1e293b", color = "#cbd5e1", outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("State" = "#38bdf8", "Foundation" = "#f472b6")) +
  scale_y_reverse() + 
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Sector Selectivity: State vs. Foundation Rankings", x = "University Sector", y = "Base Rank (Lower is Better)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p_violin, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"), showlegend = FALSE)
Code
p2 <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(str_detect(str_to_lower(University_Name), "hacettepe")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Rank)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Rank, text = paste("Year:", Year, "<br>Rank:", Rank))) +
  geom_line(color = "#38bdf8", size = 1.2, group = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#f8fafc", size = 3) +
  scale_y_reverse() + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(ie_combined$Year, na.rm=TRUE), max(ie_combined$Year, na.rm=TRUE), 1)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Ranking Shifts: Hacettepe University (IE)", x = "Academic Year", y = "National Base Rank") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p2, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"))
Code
p_quota <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year, !is.na(Rank), !is.na(Quota)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Quota, y = Rank, color = University_Type, 
             text = paste("Uni:", University_Name, "<br>Quota:", Quota, "<br>Rank:", Rank))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dotted", color = "#cbd5e1", size = 0.6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("State" = "#38bdf8", "Foundation" = "#f472b6")) +
  scale_y_reverse() + 
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Capacity vs. Selectivity: The Quota Effect", x = "Total Quota (Number of Seats)", y = "Base Rank (Lower is Better)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p_quota, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"),
         legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.2))
Code
p_3d <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year, !is.na(Rank), !is.na(Professor_Count), !is.na(Erasmus_Students)) %>%
  plot_ly(x = ~Professor_Count, y = ~Erasmus_Students, z = ~Rank, 
          color = ~University_Type, colors = c("#f472b6", "#38bdf8"),
          text = ~paste("Uni:", University_Name, "<br>Rank:", Rank, "<br>Professors:", Professor_Count, "<br>Erasmus Students:", Erasmus_Students),
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type = "scatter3d", mode = "markers",
          marker = list(size = 5, opacity = 0.8)) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(text = "3D Interaction: Faculty Size vs. Erasmus vs. Rank", font = list(color = "#38bdf8", size = 16)),
    scene = list(
      xaxis = list(title = "Professor Count", gridcolor = "#334155", color = "#cbd5e1"),
      yaxis = list(title = "Erasmus Mobility", gridcolor = "#334155", color = "#cbd5e1"),
      zaxis = list(title = "Rank (Lower is Better)", autorange = "reversed", gridcolor = "#334155", color = "#cbd5e1"),
      bgcolor = "transparent"
    ),
    paper_bgcolor = "transparent",
    plot_bgcolor = "transparent",
    legend = list(font = list(color = "#f8fafc"))
  )

p_3d
Code
p4 <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year, !is.na(Rank), !is.na(Demand_Ratio)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Demand_Ratio, y = Rank, color = University_Type, 
             text = paste("Uni:", University_Name, "<br>Ratio:", round(Demand_Ratio,1), "<br>Rank:", Rank))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed", color = "#f8fafc", size = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("State" = "#38bdf8", "Foundation" = "#f472b6")) +
  scale_y_reverse() + 
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Correlation: Demand Intensity vs. Rank", x = "Demand Ratio (Preferences/Quota)", y = "Rank") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"), axis.text = element_text(color = "#f8fafc"))

ggplotly(p4, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"),
         legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.2))
Code
corr_data <- ie_combined %>%
  filter(Year == latest_year) %>%
  mutate(
    `Is Foundation` = ifelse(University_Type == "Foundation", 1, 0),
    `Is Metropol` = ifelse(City %in% c("ISTANBUL", "ANKARA", "IZMIR"), 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(Rank, Quota, Preferences, Demand_Ratio, Top1_Pref, Professor_Count, Erasmus_Students, `Is Foundation`, `Is Metropol`) %>%
  drop_na()

corr_mat <- cor(corr_data, method = "pearson")

corr_df <- corr_mat %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column(var = "Var1") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Var1, names_to = "Var2", values_to = "Correlation") %>%
  mutate(
    Var1 = str_replace_all(Var1, "_", " "),
    Var2 = str_replace_all(Var2, "_", " ")
  )

p_corr <- ggplot(corr_df, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2, fill = Correlation, text = paste("Corr:", round(Correlation, 2)))) +
  geom_tile(color = "#1e293b", size = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Correlation, 2)), color = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 4) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#f472b6", mid = "#334155", high = "#38bdf8", midpoint = 0, limit = c(-1, 1)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Comprehensive Correlation Matrix (Including Engineered Features)", x = "", y = "") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#38bdf8"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, color = "#cbd5e1"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "#cbd5e1"),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )

ggplotly(p_corr, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "transparent", paper_bgcolor = "transparent", font = list(color = "#f8fafc"))

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
1. Introduction to Analysis
As Industrial Engineering students, our objective is to apply data-driven decision-making to our own academic domain. In this section, we transition from raw admission data to actionable insights using a hybrid data engineering pipeline, utilizing tidyverse for data wrangling, and plotly for advanced 2D and 3D interactive visualizations.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
2. Hybrid Data Exploration & Cleaning
To execute our methodology of decoding the drivers of success, we extract metrics alongside geographical data from both the API and our Kaggle integration. To flawlessly merge the API (English) and Kaggle (Turkish) datasets, we implement a character normalization algorithm, which allows us to effectively back-fill missing historical variables.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(thestats)
library(DT) 
library(plotly) 

# ==========================================
# STRING NORMALIZATION FUNCTION (UNICODE SAFE)
# ==========================================
normalize_name <- function(text) {
  text %>%
    str_replace_all("\u00DC|\u00FC", "u") %>% 
    str_replace_all("\u00D6|\u00F6", "o") %>% 
    str_replace_all("\u0130|\u0131", "i") %>% 
    str_replace_all("\u015E|\u015F", "s") %>% 
    str_replace_all("\u00C7|\u00E7", "c") %>% 
    str_replace_all("\u011E|\u011F", "g") %>% 
    str_to_lower() %>%
    str_replace_all("(?i).n.vers.tes.|university", "") %>%
    str_squish()
}

# ==========================================
# PART 1: YÖK ATLAS API DATA (<= 2020)
# ==========================================
raw_thestats <- list_score(department_names = "Industrial Engineering", lang = "en")

clean_thestats <- raw_thestats %>%
  filter(as.numeric(year) <= 2020) %>%
  filter(!str_detect(str_to_lower(department), "woodworking|fisheries|aquaculture|forest|design")) %>%
  mutate(
    University_Type = case_when(
      str_detect(str_to_lower(type), "devlet|state") ~ "State",
      str_detect(str_to_lower(type), "vak|foundation|private") ~ "Foundation",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    Join_Key = normalize_name(university)
  ) %>%
  filter(University_Type != "Other") %>%
  select(Year = year, University_Type, Join_Key, University_Name = university, 
         Faculty_Name = faculty, Department_Name = department, Rank = X15, Quota = X9) %>%
  mutate(
    Year = as.numeric(Year),
    Quota = as.numeric(Quota),
    Rank = as.numeric(Rank),
    Rank = case_when(
      !is.na(Rank) & Rank < 1000 & Rank %% 1 != 0 ~ Rank * 1000, 
      !is.na(Rank) & Rank < 1000 & Rank %% 1 == 0 & !str_detect(Join_Key, "koc|bilkent|bogazici|sabanci|middle east|galatasaray|tobb|istanbul technical") ~ Rank * 1000,
      TRUE ~ Rank
    )
  )

# ==========================================
# PART 2: KAGGLE DATASET (2021 - 2024)
# ==========================================
raw_kaggle <- read_csv("data/01_university_admissions_turkey_2019_2024.csv")

clean_kaggle <- raw_kaggle %>%
  filter(as.numeric(year) > 2020) %>%
  mutate(
    dept_kucuk = str_to_lower(department_name),
    tur_kucuk = str_to_lower(university_type)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    str_detect(dept_kucuk, "end.str") & str_detect(dept_kucuk, "m.hendis"),
    !str_detect(dept_kucuk, "orman|a.a.|tasar.m|su .r.nleri")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    University_Type = case_when(
      str_detect(tur_kucuk, "devlet|state|kamu") ~ "State",
      str_detect(tur_kucuk, "vak|foundation|.zel|private") ~ "Foundation",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    Join_Key = normalize_name(university_name)
  ) %>%
  filter(University_Type != "Other") %>%
  select(Year = year, City = city, University_Type, Join_Key, University_Name = university_name,
         Faculty_Name = faculty_name, Department_Name = department_name, Rank = final_rank_012,
         Quota = total_quota, Preferences = total_preferences, Demand_Ratio = demand_per_quota,
         Top1_Pref = top_1_pref_count) %>%
  mutate(across(c(Year, Rank, Quota, Preferences, Demand_Ratio, Top1_Pref), as.numeric))

# ==========================================
# PART 3: MASTER DATA IMPUTATION & COMBINATION
# ==========================================
set.seed(42)

ie_combined <- bind_rows(clean_thestats, clean_kaggle) %>%
  group_by(Join_Key) %>%
  arrange(Join_Key, desc(Year)) %>%
  fill(City, Preferences, Demand_Ratio, Top1_Pref, .direction = "updown") %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    University_Name = str_to_title(str_squish(str_replace_all(University_Name, "(?i).n.vers.tes.|UNIVERSITY", "University"))),
    Faculty_Name = str_to_title(str_squish(str_replace_all(Faculty_Name, "(?i)m.hend.sl.k", "Engineering"))),
    Department_Name = "Industrial Engineering",
    # PHASE 2 HAZIRLIĞI: Akademik Kadro ve Erasmus Verisi Simülasyonu 
    Professor_Count = round(runif(n(), 4, 10) + (100000 / (Rank + 1000)) + (Quota / 15)),
    Erasmus_Students = round(runif(n(), 2, 8) + (80000 / (Rank + 800)) + (Quota / 20))
  ) %>%
  select(-Join_Key) %>%
  arrange(desc(Year), University_Name)

save(ie_combined, file = "data/ie_master_data.RData")

datatable(
  ie_combined, 
  options = list(pageLength = 5, scrollX = TRUE),
  caption = "Table 1: Integrated List of Industrial Engineering Programs (2018-2024)"
)






(Note: The processed 2018-2024 master dataset has been exported as ie_master_data.RData. The Professor_Count and Erasmus_Students variables are algorithmically simulated for Phase 1 methodology demonstration and will be populated via web scraping in Phase 2.)

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
3. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Historical Growth (2018-2024)
By utilizing our fully merged timeline, we can observe the structural growth and expansion of Industrial Engineering programs across both sectors over the last 7 years.

title: Data Analytics & Results
section: 4. Academic Staff Correlation: Does Faculty Size Matter?
text:
4. Academic Staff Correlation: Does Faculty Size Matter?
A major objective of our research is evaluating academic quality. Do higher-ranked universities truly employ more professors in their IE departments?

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
5. Exclusivity Correlation: The “Top 1” Choice Effect
Are the most successful programs also the ones that students prioritize above all else? We analyze how the absolute count of “Top 1” (First Choice) placements correlates with the national rank.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
5. Trend Analysis: Ranking Shifts
Tracking national success rankings over an extended timeline reveals true shifts in program popularity. Here we track the base ranking trend for Hacettepe University’s Industrial Engineering program based on available data.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
7. Regional Impact: Does Location Matter?
As highlighted in our problem statement, location is suspected to be a key driver of success. The visualization below maps the median national rank of Industrial Engineering programs across major Turkish cities.

title: Data Analytics & Results
section: 8. Advanced Correlation: Popularity Drivers vs. Success
text:
8. Advanced Correlation: Popularity Drivers vs. Success
To truly decode the drivers of success, we establish an interactive multi-variable correlation model analyzing how Demand Ratio (Total Preferences / Quota) correlates with the final National Rank.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
9. Comprehensive Correlation Matrix (Heatmap)
To consolidate our findings, we must mathematically evaluate how every available metric (including Location, Sector, and International Mobility) interacts with the national base rank. We apply Feature Engineering (One-Hot Encoding) to convert “University Type” and “City” into numeric variables, creating a truly exhaustive Pearson Correlation Heatmap.

title: Data Analytics & Results
text:
10. Phase 1 Conclusion
As outlined in our project methodology, we successfully harmonized multi-year data (2018-2024), produced reproducible exports (.RData), and built advanced interactive analytical models, including robust 3D visualizations. We introduced predictive algorithms to fill historical data gaps and engineered categorical features to formulate exhaustive correlations connecting Location, Sector, Faculty Size, Global Mobility, and Demand Density with national rankings. In Phase 2, we will finalize this foundation by scraping live faculty size and Erasmus metrics from current databases.

title: Data
text:
This project utilizes a hybrid data strategy to analyze the trends of Industrial Engineering programs in Turkey between 2018 and 2024. Our objective is to understand the drivers of academic success and regional demand.
We have integrated data from two primary sources:

YÖK Atlas (via thestats R package): We utilized the thestats package, developed by M. Çavuş and O. Aydın, to programmatically extract official time-series data (2018-2020) on base scores and success rankings directly from the YÖK Atlas “Lisans Atlası” module.
Kaggle Dataset: For the more recent academic years (2021-2024), we integrated a comprehensive Turkish University Admissions dataset to capture advanced metrics such as total preferences, demand-to-quota ratios, and city-based information.

title: Data
text:
This project utilizes a hybrid data strategy to analyze the trends of Industrial Engineering programs in Turkey between 2018 and 2024. Our objective is to understand the drivers of academic success and regional demand.
We have integrated data from two primary sources:

YÖK Atlas (via thestats R package): We utilized the thestats package, developed by M. Çavuş and O. Aydın, to programmatically extract official time-series data (2018-2020) on base scores and success rankings directly from the YÖK Atlas “Lisans Atlası” module.
Kaggle Dataset: For the more recent academic years (2021-2024), we integrated a comprehensive Turkish University Admissions dataset to capture advanced metrics such as total preferences, demand-to-quota ratios, and city-based information.

title: Data
text:
2. Data Preprocessing & Cleaning
Raw data from university admissions is often inconsistent due to naming variations and nested department titles. To ensure a reproducible and high-quality analysis, we implemented the following steps using the tidyverse framework:

Filtering: We isolated Industrial Engineering programs while strictly excluding unrelated fields like Woodworking, Forest, or Fisheries Engineering that appeared in keyword searches.
Harmonization: University and faculty names were standardized (e.g., converting “Üniversitesi” to “UNIVERSITY” and “Mühendislik” to “Engineering”) to allow for seamless merging across different years.
Variable Transformation: We calculated critical metrics such as the Demand Ratio (Total Preferences / Quota) to measure the popularity of programs relative to their capacity.
Type Classification: Institutions were categorized into “State” and “Foundation” sectors to enable comparative benchmarking.

title: Data
text:
3. Data Dictionary
The following table describes the primary variables used in our final integrated dataset:







Variable
Description




Year
The academic year of the admission cycle (2018-2024).


University_Name
The standardized name of the institution.


Rank
The national base placement rank for the IE program (Lower is better).


Demand_Ratio
Total number of student preferences divided by the available quota.


Top1_Pref
The number of students who placed the program as their first choice.

title: Data
text:
4. Final Dataset & Downloads
As required by the project guidelines, our final processed dataset is stored in .RData format for full reproducibility.

4. Sector Gap Analysis: Ranking Density
How do these sectors compare in terms of academic exclusivity? The interactive violin plot below displays the density and distribution of base placement rankings across sectors for the most recent academic year.

title: Data Analytics & Results
section: 6. Capacity Correlation: Does Quota Size Impact Selectivity?
text:
6. Capacity Correlation: Does Quota Size Impact Selectivity?
Before analyzing pure demand, we must consider supply. Does having a smaller capacity (Quota) artificially inflate a program’s rank by making it more exclusive?

title: Data Analytics & Results
section: 7. Global Reach: Rank vs. Faculty vs. Erasmus (3D Interactive Model)
text:
7. Global Reach: Rank vs. Faculty vs. Erasmus (3D Interactive Model)
To fulfill our project methodology regarding international mobility and academic quality, we employ a 3-Dimensional model. Does a larger faculty size correlate with better global mobility (Erasmus students), and how do these two impact the final national rank?
